3. Directus - Headless CMS, data management
  4. NocoDB - Airtable alternative, self-hosted
  5. Baserow - Airtable alternative

  Given their use case (crypto investigation platform with lots of data), PocketBase would be amazing - it's one file, h
  as auth, real-time subscriptions, file storage, and admin UI. Perfect for rapid prototyping and even production.

  Or they might mean Replit specifically - which has "Replit Deployments" now and free tier.

  Let me give them the best options for self-hosted data management that's cheaper than managed solutions.
• Ah, you're probably thinking of PocketBase or Appwrite - or maybe Replit itself. Let me break down the best self-hoste
  d data management options:
  🏆 TOP PICKS FOR SELF-HOSTED DATA
  1. PocketBase ⭐ (Probably what you want)
  • What: Single-file backend (Go binary)
  • Database: SQLite (or PostgreSQL)
  • Features: Auth, real-time, file storage, admin dashboard
  • Cost: $0 (self-hosted)
  • Size: Single ~20MB executable
  • Perfect for: Your investigation data, user management, file uploads
  # Deploy anywhere in 30 seconds
  ./pocketbase serve
  # Done - API + Admin UI running
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  2. Appwrite (Open Source Firebase)
  • What: Full backend-as-a-service
  • Database: MongoDB or MariaDB
  • Features: Auth, storage, functions, real-time
  • Cost: $0 self-hosted
  • Good for: More complex than PocketBase, but still self-hosted
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  3. NocoDB (Airtable Alternative)
  • What: Turn any database into smart spreadsheet
  • Connects to: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite
  • Perfect for: Viewing investigation data in spreadsheet format
  • Cost: $0
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  4. Directus (Headless CMS)
  • What: Data management layer on top of SQL
  • Beautiful admin interface
  • Good for: Managing complex data relationships
  • Cost: $0 self-hosted
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

6. DRAGONFLY (The Redis Replacement)
Replaces: Redis (single-threaded, limited to 1 CPU core)
The Free Tier:
Open source, drop-in Redis replacement
Multi-threaded: Uses all 8 cores of your Contabo server (Redis uses 1)
The Game-Changer:
Vertical scaling: 10x throughput on same hardware. Handle 10,000 concurrent wallet risk score queries vs 1,000 in Redis.
Snapshots without blocking: Backup your hot wallet cache without freezing the API (Redis blocks for seconds during BGSAVE)
Your Use Case:
Cache wallet reputation scores (hot data). 100K wallets in memory, queried 10K times/second during market volatility.
Integration: redis-cli commands work unchanged. Change n8n Redis connection from localhost:6379 (Redis) to localhost:6379 (Dragonfly), get 10x performance.

4. SCYLLADB (The Cassandra Killer)
Replaces: Redis for large datasets (Redis keeps everything in RAM, Scylla uses disk + RAM cache)
The Free Tier:
Open source, self-hosted
10x faster than Cassandra, handles millions of writes/second on a single node
The Game-Changer:
Write-heavy blockchain ingestion: Solana produces 5MB/s of data. Scylla ingests this on a $10/month VPS without breaking a sweat (Redis would OOM).
Time-to-Live (TTL): Auto-delete wallet data after 90 days (compliance), but keep risk scores forever. Automated data lifecycle.
Materialized Views: Pre-compute "top 100 scammer wallets by volume" in real-time as data arrives (no batch jobs)
Your Use Case:
Store every Solana transaction signature (500M+ records) on disk, query recent ones in RAM—impossible with Redis alone on limited hardware
Integration: CQL protocol (Cassandra-compatible), n8n has native Cassandra node.
1. Deploy Scylla now (for future-proofing)?

5. TEMPORAL (The Workflow Engine)
Replaces: n8n for critical financial workflows (n8n is great, but not "durable" for money-moving operations)
The Free Tier:
Temporal Cloud: 5,000,000 actions/month free (generous)
Self-hosted on Contabo: Unlimited (Docker compose)
The Game-Changer:
Durable Execution: If your server crashes mid-way through a "buyback and burn" workflow, Temporal resumes exactly where it left off when you reboot (n8n would lose the state and double-spend)
Sagas: Multi-step transactions (e.g., "Buy token on DEX → Wait 6 hours → Burn → Update database") that automatically rollback if any step fails
Cron with Guarantees: Your "Daily Degen Intelligence Report" runs at exactly 6:00 AM UTC—even if the server was down at 5:59, it runs immediately on recovery
Your Use Case:
TypeScript
Copy
// Workflow for "Emergency Buyback" that CANNOT fail or double-execute
const buybackWorkflow = async () => {
  await executeChild('checkTreasuryBalance');  // Step 1
  await sleep('24h');                          // Step 2: Wait for timelock
  await executeChild('executeBuyback');        // Step 3: If this fails, retry 3x then alert
  await executeChild('verifyBurn');            // Step 4: Must confirm on-chain before finalizing
}
// If server dies during Step 2, Temporal resumes at Step 2 with remaining sleep time
Integration: Replaces n8n only for financial-critical workflows (treasury management). Keep n8n for data scraping (it's better there).

Here is the step-by-step architectural blueprint to build this investigation app using the resources you have.

Phase 1: Infrastructure Setup
Note: I assume "cantato" refers to Contabo, which is excellent for cost-effective, high-RAM virtual private servers.

Contabo Server (The Brain & Storage): Use this to host your local models and your Vector Database. Install Ollama here to run smaller, local LLMs (like Llama 3 8B) for fast, free data extraction and categorization. Install ChromaDB or Qdrant as your Vector Database to hold your RAG data.

AWS ($200 Credit - Data Lake & App Hosting): Use Amazon S3 to store your raw data dumps safely. You can also use AWS EC2 or AppRunner to host the actual user interface of your app so you can access it anywhere.

OpenRouter / DeepSeek: Keep these API keys ready for the "heavy lifting"—complex reasoning, connecting the dots, and generating the final investigative reports.

Phase 2: Data Ingestion & The RAG Pipeline
Before the bot can analyze anything, it needs organized access to your raw dumps (Telegram logs, Discord chats, CSVs of transactions, code snippets).

Extract & Clean: Write a Python script to pull your raw data from AWS S3, strip out junk formatting, and convert it to plain text.

Categorization (Local LLM): Have your local Contabo LLM read each chunk of data and tag it (e.g., [Wallet Address], [Social Handle], [Transaction Hash]).

Embed & Store: Pass the cleaned text through an embedding model (like nomic-embed-text via Ollama) to turn the text into searchable numbers. Store these in your Vector Database.

Phase 3: The Blockchain Verification Engine
You need to build Python functions (often called "Tools" in AI terminology) that your LLM can trigger when it finds a wallet address.

Tool 1: Status & Balance Checker (Helius / RPCs): In crypto, wallets don't "close," they just get drained to 0 balance or their private keys are abandoned. This tool queries Helius (if $CRM is on Solana) to check the current balance and the timestamp of the last transaction.

Tool 2: The Money Tracer (Arkham API): If Tool 1 shows the money moved out, this tool queries Arkham to follow the outgoing transaction hashes. It maps the flow of funds to see if it hits a Centralized Exchange (like Binance or Kraken) or a mixer (like Tornado Cash).

Tool 3: Entity Labeler: Uses Arkham to check if a wallet is already flagged as a known scammer, a bridge, or a specific exchange deposit address.

Phase 4: Agent Orchestration (The Logic)
You will need an orchestration framework to tie the LLM to your RAG and APIs. LangChain or LlamaIndex (using Python) are the industry standards for this.

Your agent's workflow will look like this:

Trigger: You press a button: "Analyze wallet 0xABC..."

Retrieve: The RAG searches your Vector DB for any mentions of 0xABC in your Discord/Telegram dumps.

Execute: The Agent sends 0xABC to the Arkham/Helius APIs to get the real-time blockchain truth.

Synthesize: The Agent bundles the RAG context and the API data, sends it to DeepSeek via OpenRouter, and asks: "Based on chat logs and blockchain data, who owns this, and where did the funds go?"

Phase 5: The User Interface (Buttons & Dashboards)
To get the "categorize into buttons for me and press" experience, do not build a complex web app from scratch. Use Streamlit.

Streamlit is a Python library that turns data scripts into shareable web apps in minutes. You can easily build:

A sidebar to upload new data dumps.

A central dashboard showing a list of suspicious wallets.

A literal "Trace Funds" button next to each wallet that triggers your LangChain agent and displays the output.

AWS Glue
This is the primary data preparation service. You drop your raw transaction logs or JSON files into Amazon S3, and AWS Glue sends out Crawlers. These automatically scan the files, figure out the underlying structure, and organize the information into a neat Data Catalog. It essentially turns a messy pile of files into a fully queryable database.

Amazon Comprehend
If your evidence includes massive walls of text like exported Telegram chats, this Natural Language Processing service is incredibly useful. It reads unstructured text and automatically pulls out entities. You can train it to specifically recognize and extract wallet addresses, names, and transaction hashes from endless chat logs.

Amazon Textract
For visual evidence like screenshots of deleted posts or PDF documents, this service extracts text and tables directly from the images and converts them into structured data formats.

Handling multiple documents—even thousands of them—is exactly what this architecture is built for. You won't be processing these one by one; you will set up a pipeline that ingests them in bulk.

Here is how you handle a massive dump of multiple documents across your AWS and RAG setup:

1. The "Data Lake" Approach (Amazon S3)
Instead of dealing with individual files, you create an Amazon S3 Bucket (think of it as a master folder in the cloud). You dump all your documents in there. You can organize them into sub-folders like s3://crm-investigation/telegram-chats/, s3://crm-investigation/transaction-csvs/, and s3://crm-investigation/screenshots/.

2. Batch Processing on AWS
AWS services are designed to process entire buckets of files at once:

AWS Glue: You point a Glue Crawler at your transaction-csvs folder. Even if there are 500 different CSV files from different block explorers, Glue will scan all of them and combine them into a single, massive, searchable database table.

Textract & Comprehend (Asynchronous Batching): If you have 1,000 screenshots or PDFs, you don't send them to Textract one at a time. You start an Asynchronous Job. You tell AWS, "Here is a folder with 1,000 images; process them all and put the extracted text into this other folder." It does this in the background, saving you immense time.

3. Ingesting into your AI (The RAG Pipeline)
Once AWS has converted your multiple files (PDFs, images, raw logs) into clean text, you move them to your Contabo server to feed your Vector Database. Here is how LangChain handles multiple files:

Directory Loaders: Instead of loading a single file, you use a script that sweeps a whole folder. It reads every document automatically.

Chunking: The script breaks every document down into smaller "chunks" (usually 500 to 1,000 words). If you have a 50-page chat log, it becomes roughly 50 chunks.

Metadata Tagging (Crucial Step): As it breaks down the files, the script attaches metadata to every chunk. It tags the chunk with the original filename, the date, and the source.

Why Metadata matters for your App
When your bot eventually does the research, you want it to cite its sources. Because you tagged the metadata during the bulk upload, when you click the "Trace Funds" button, the LLM will output:

"Wallet 0xABC received 50,000 $CRM on Tuesday. Source: [Discord_Log_Archive_4.txt, Line 405]. This wallet was later flagged as a Kraken deposit address. Source: [Arkham API]."

Summary of the Workflow for Multiple Files:
Dump everything into an AWS S3 bucket.

Run AWS Glue to unify spreadsheets, and Textract to pull text from images in bulk.

Download the cleaned text to your Contabo server.

Run a Python script (LangChain) to chop all the text into chunks, tag them with filenames, and embed them into your Vector Database.

Your chatbot now has instant access to every single piece of evidence simultaneously.

You tell DeepSeek:

DeepSeek does the heavy lifting of reading the chaos and returning perfectly clean data.

5. Auto-Filing into your Database
Your Python script takes that clean JSON output from DeepSeek and automatically files it in two places:

The Vector DB (Chroma/Qdrant): The text goes here so your bot can search it later for the RAG pipeline.

A Relational Database: The extracted wallet addresses and transaction hashes go into a structured table. This is what eventually powers the win rate scorecards on your app's frontend.

By setting it up this way, your app does the organization for you. You just upload a messy folder, and five minutes later, all the wallets are neatly categorized into buttons on your dashboard, ready to be investigated.

1. Shift All AI Work to the APIs
Instead of running a model locally to rename files and extract text, your Python script will send those API calls directly to OpenRouter or DeepSeek.

Cost Factor: DeepSeek and smaller models on OpenRouter (like Llama 3 8B or Claude Haiku) cost mere fractions of a cent per thousand tokens. You can process and rename thousands of chaotic files through their APIs for pennies.

2. Contabo's New Role: The Traffic Cop
Your Contabo server is relieved of heavy AI processing. It now only handles tasks that rely on CPU and RAM, which it excels at:

Running the Python Script: The script that watches your AWS S3 bucket, hashes the files, and talks to the DeepSeek API.

Hosting the Vector Database: Databases like ChromaDB or Qdrant do not need graphics cards. They are highly optimized to run text searches using just standard CPU and RAM.

Hosting the UI: Running the Streamlit web app where you press your investigation buttons.

The Revised Workflow
File drops in AWS S3.

Contabo Python script hashes it to give it an ID.

Contabo script sends the first page of text to DeepSeek API and asks for a clean file name.

DeepSeek API replies with Discord_Chat_0xABC.

Contabo script saves the text and metadata into the local ChromaDB.

This keeps your infrastructure fast, cheap, and strictly focused on the investigation without melting your server.

Having access to Arkham, Helius, and Solscan APIs is what turns this from a basic text search engine into a forensic powerhouse. The raw data dumps (chats, logs) are the claims, but the blockchain APIs are the mathematical proof.

To make this work seamlessly, you integrate these APIs at two specific stages in your pipeline: Data Enrichment (when you upload files) and Agent Tools (when you press the investigate button).

Here is exactly how they fit into the architecture.

Stage 1: Auto-Enrichment (During File Upload)
You don't just want to store the chaotic text; you want to verify it immediately.

Extract: When your Contabo script sends a chaotic file to DeepSeek to get a clean title, it also gives DeepSeek a second instruction: "Extract every wallet address and transaction hash from this text."

Ping APIs: Your script takes those extracted addresses and automatically fires them off to your blockchain APIs in the background.

Arkham API: Checks if the wallet is already labeled (e.g., "Binance Deposit," "Known Phishing Scammer").

Solscan / Helius API: Pulls the exact timestamp of the transaction, the token amounts, and the current live balance.

Merge and Store: Your script bundles the original chat log, the DeepSeek clean title, AND the verified API data into one chunk. It saves this enriched package into your ChromaDB. Now, your database doesn't just know what was said in the chats; it already knows if the wallet associated with that chat is flagged by Arkham.

Stage 2: Agent Tool Calling (The Investigation Phase)
This is where your DeepSeek/OpenRouter models act as an investigator. Modern LLMs support a feature called Function Calling (or Tool Use). You essentially give the AI the instruction manual for your Arkham and Helius APIs.

When you press the "Trace Funds" button on your dashboard, here is what the AI does autonomously:

Search the RAG: The LLM pulls the enriched data from your ChromaDB (e.g., it finds the Telegram log where the scammer posted wallet A).

Use the Helius Tool: The LLM realizes, "I need to know where the $CRM went from wallet A." It automatically writes the code to query the Helius API for wallet A's recent transfers and executes it.

Use the Arkham Tool: Helius replies that the funds moved to wallet B. The LLM then automatically queries the Arkham API: "Who owns wallet B?" Arkham replies: "Wallet B is a KuCoin hot wallet."

The Final Output: The AI stops, synthesizes everything, and gives you a clean report:

"According to Telegram logs, the scammer used Wallet A. Helius data confirms 500,000 $CRM was moved to Wallet B on Tuesday. Arkham data identifies Wallet B as a KuCoin deposit address. The funds have been cashed out to an exchange."

Summary of API Roles
Helius (RPC/Webhooks): Your fast, raw data layer. Use it to check exact Solana transaction paths, token account states, and whether a wallet has been drained to zero.

Solscan: Good for historical lookups and parsing complex smart contract interactions if the scammer used custom programs to drain the $CRM.

Arkham: Your intelligence layer. Use it strictly for entity resolution—finding out if an anonymous address actually belongs to a centralized exchange, a mixer, or a known threat actor.

more features closed accounts

In the world of blockchain, a "closed account" is a very specific type of evidence. Wallets don't really have a "delete account" button like a social media profile.

Instead, a closed account usually means one of three things:

Drained & Abandoned (Burner Wallets): The scammer moved the $CRM out and left the balance at absolutely zero.

Burned Token Accounts (Solana specific): Scammers often use a CloseAccount command to delete the temporary token accounts they used, usually to reclaim the tiny fraction of SOL associated with it.

Frozen/Blacklisted (CEX or Stablecoins): The funds hit a centralized exchange (like Binance) or were converted to Tether/USDC, and law enforcement or the exchange froze the account.

Since you already have a ghost_scan.py in your folder structure, this is the perfect place to build out features specifically designed to handle these "dead ends."

Here are the advanced features we can build into your Telegram bot to handle closed accounts:

1. The "Gas Trail" Tracker (Tracing Backward)
When you hit a burner wallet that has a zero balance and zero recent activity, forward-tracing is useless. You must trace backward.

The Feature: You press [👻 Scan Ghost Wallet] in Telegram.

The Logic: Your ghost_scan.py script queries Helius or Solscan specifically for the very first transaction that wallet ever made.

Why it works: Scammers need gas (SOL or ETH) to pay for their first transactions. They almost always fund their burner wallets from a central "funding wallet" or a centralized exchange. Finding the funding source often rips the mask off the syndicate.

2. The "Rent Reclaim" Trap (Solana Specific)
If $CRM is on Solana, scammers love to be greedy. When they empty a wallet, they often close the associated token accounts to get the "rent" (about 0.002 SOL) refunded to their main wallet.

The Feature: Your app looks for the CloseAccount instruction in the blockchain data.

The Logic: When the Librarian spots this in the raw data, it flags the destination address where that 0.002 SOL was sent. That destination is almost always the scammer’s actual, permanent vault.

3. The Freeze Checker (Blacklist Ping)
If the scammer swapped $CRM for USDC or USDT, you need to know if the authorities have already caught them.

The Feature: A script that automatically checks the Tether and Circle (USDC) smart contracts.

The Logic: It pings the contract to see if the scammer's wallet address has been added to the global Blacklist. If it has, the funds are frozen, and your app updates the locker.py file to mark them as "FROZEN_CRIMINAL".

4. The CEX "Subpoena Generator"
When your analysis/laundry.py script proves the funds went from a burner wallet into a Centralized Exchange (like Kraken or KuCoin), the trail goes cold for you. Only the exchange knows who owns that account.

The Feature: You press [📝 Generate Legal Request] in Telegram.

The Logic: DeepSeek takes all the structured data from your database (the source wallets, the exact transaction hashes, the timestamps, and the exchange deposit address) and automatically types up a formalized Law Enforcement Request or Incident Report PDF. You can then immediately hand this clean, professional document to cyber crimes divisions or exchange support teams to get that specific account officially frozen.

money trail tracker

Tracing the original gas funding is the ultimate reality check for scammers. No matter how many ghost wallets or hops they use to hide the $CRM, they had to pay SOL gas fees to open that very first burner wallet.

Finding that first funding transaction almost always leads directly back to a Binance or KuCoin hot wallet, which gives you the exact timestamp to subpoena.

Here is the code to build the "Gas Trail" Tracker. We will put this in your analysis/ghost_scan.py file. It uses the Helius API to bypass the recent noise, page all the way back to the wallet's genesis, and find out exactly who funded it.

Step 1: Update ghost_scan.py

Python
import requests
import time

HELIUS_API_KEY = "YOUR_HELIUS_API_KEY"

def trace_gas_funding(ghost_wallet_address):
    """
    Pages backward through a wallet's entire history to find the 
    very first transaction, revealing the original funding source.
    """
    print(f"[*] Initiating Ghost Scan on {ghost_wallet_address}...")
    
    url = f"https://api.helius.xyz/v0/addresses/{ghost_wallet_address}/transactions?api-key={HELIUS_API_KEY}"
    
    # We want to page backward to the oldest transaction
    last_signature = None
    oldest_tx = None
    
    # Loop through transaction history until we hit the beginning
    while True:
        params = {}
        if last_signature:
            params['before'] = last_signature
            
        response = requests.get(url, params=params)
        
        if response.status_code != 200:
            return {"error": f"Helius API error: {response.status_code}"}
            
        transactions = response.json()
        
        # If no more transactions, we've hit the genesis of the wallet
        if not transactions:
            break
            
        # Keep track of the oldest we've seen so far
        oldest_tx = transactions[-1] 
        last_signature = oldest_tx['signature']
        time.sleep(0.5) # Prevent rate limiting

    if not oldest_tx:
        return {"error": "No transactions found for this wallet."}

    # Analyze the genesis transaction
    tx_type = oldest_tx.get('type')
    timestamp = oldest_tx.get('timestamp')
    
    # Find who sent the native SOL to fund this wallet
    funder = None
    amount_funded = 0
    
    if 'nativeTransfers' in oldest_tx:
        for transfer in oldest_tx['nativeTransfers']:
            if transfer['toUserAccount'] == ghost_wallet_address:
                funder = transfer['fromUserAccount']
                amount_funded = transfer['amount'] / 1_000_000_000 # Convert Lamports to SOL
                break

    return {
        "status": "ORIGIN_FOUND",
        "ghost_wallet": ghost_wallet_address,
        "funded_by": funder,
        "amount_sol": amount_funded,
        "timestamp": timestamp,
        "genesis_signature": oldest_tx['signature']
    }

# Quick test if you run this script directly
if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_wallet = "ENTER_A_SUSPECT_WALLET_HERE"
    result = trace_gas_funding(test_wallet)
    print(result)

import json
import os
from datetime import datetime

DOSSIER_FILE = "suspect_dossiers.json"

def create_or_update_suspect(suspect_id, alias=None, wallet=None, timezone=None, note=None):
    """Adds new intelligence to a human suspect's dossier."""
    data = {}
    if os.path.exists(DOSSIER_FILE):
        with open(DOSSIER_FILE, "r") as f:
            try:
                data = json.load(f)
            except:
                data = {}

    # Create new profile if it doesn't exist
    if suspect_id not in data:
        data[suspect_id] = {
            "aliases": [],
            "wallets": [],
            "estimated_timezone": "Unknown",
            "behavioral_notes": [],
            "status": "UNDER_INVESTIGATION",
            "last_updated": str(datetime.now())
        }

    profile = data[suspect_id]

    # Append new evidence without duplicating
    if alias and alias not in profile["aliases"]:
        profile["aliases"].append(alias)
    if wallet and wallet not in profile["wallets"]:
        profile["wallets"].append(wallet)
    if timezone:
        profile["estimated_timezone"] = timezone
    if note:
        profile["behavioral_notes"].append(note)

    profile["last_updated"] = str(datetime.now())

    with open(DOSSIER_FILE, "w") as f:
        json.dump(data, f, indent=4)
    
    return profile

def get_directory():
    """Returns the list of all suspects for the Telegram UI."""
    if not os.path.exists(DOSSIER_FILE):
        return {}
    with open(DOSSIER_FILE, "r") as f:
        return json.load(f)

# Quick Test
if __name__ == "__main__":
    create_or_update_suspect("Target_Alpha", alias="@CRM_Dev", wallet="0xXYZ")
    print("Directory Updated.")

show manipulation of token over time

To prove token manipulation over time—and to make it verifiable so Claude or a defense attorney cannot poke holes in it—we cannot rely on vibes or chat logs. We need mathematical proof of Wash Trading or Circular Volume.

When scammers launch a token like $CRM, they often use a cluster of 5 to 10 wallets that trade back and forth with each other to create fake volume, tricking the algorithms into putting the token on the "Trending" lists.

Here is how we build the manipulation.py module to track this over time, generate a timeline, and send a visual report to your Telegram bot.

The Verification Logic (How it works)
Your script will query the Helius or Birdeye API for the token's entire trade history. It will then look for two mathematically verifiable red flags:

Zero-Sum Sybil Trading: Wallet A buys 10,000 $CRM, transfers it to Wallet B, and Wallet B sells exactly 10,000 $CRM 5 minutes later. The net change is zero, but it generated fake volume.

High-Frequency Wash Rings: A small cluster of wallets making up over 50% of the daily transaction volume, trading strictly with each other.

The Code (modules/manipulation.py)
Open your terminal and edit the manipulation module:

Bash
nano modules/manipulation.py
Paste this logic. This script simulates analyzing the blockchain data and structuring it into a timeline:

Python
import json
from collections import defaultdict
from datetime import datetime

def analyze_volume_manipulation(token_address, trade_data):
    """
    Analyzes raw trade data to detect circular wash trading over time.
    trade_data: A list of dicts from Helius/Birdeye containing trades.
    """
    print(f"[*] Auditing temporal volume for {token_address}...")
    
    # Trackers for our evidence
    daily_volume = defaultdict(float)
    wash_volume = defaultdict(float)
    suspect_wallets = set()
    
    # A simplified logic loop to detect manipulation
    for trade in trade_data:
        date = trade['timestamp'].split('T')[0] # Get just the YYYY-MM-DD
        volume = trade['amount_usd']
        wallet = trade['wallet']
        
        daily_volume[date] += volume
        
        # VERIFICATION FLAG: Does this wallet buy and sell within seconds?
        # Or does it trade exclusively with another flagged wallet?
        if trade.get('is_circular') or trade.get('time_in_position_seconds', 999) < 60:
            wash_volume[date] += volume
            suspect_wallets.add(wallet)

    # Generate the Timeline Report
    report = f"📈 **MANIPULATION TIMELINE: {token_address}** 📈\n\n"
    total_fake = 0
    total_real = 0
    
    for date in sorted(daily_volume.keys()):
        real_vol = daily_volume[date] - wash_volume[date]
        fake_vol = wash_volume[date]
        total_real += real_vol
        total_fake += fake_vol
        
        manipulation_percent = (fake_vol / daily_volume[date]) * 100 if daily_volume[date] > 0 else 0
        
        report += f"**{date}:**\n"
        report += f"  - Total Volume: ${daily_volume[date]:,.2f}\n"
        report += f"  - Wash/Fake Volume: ${fake_vol:,.2f} ({manipulation_percent:.1f}%)\n"
        
        # Add a visual ASCII warning if manipulation is high
        if manipulation_percent > 40:
            report += f"  - 🚨 ALERT: Severe Wash Trading Detected\n"

    report += f"\n**SUMMARY:**\n"
    report += f"Identified **{len(suspect_wallets)} Sybil Wallets** operating a wash ring.\n"
    report += f"Total Artificial Volume: ${total_fake:,.2f} / Total Legitimate Volume: ${total_real:,.2f}\n"

    return {
        "status": "MANIPULATION_CONFIRMED" if total_fake > (total_real * 0.2) else "CLEAN",
        "sybil_wallets": list(suspect_wallets),
        "telegram_report": report
    }

# Quick Test Example
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Mock data representing what your Helius/Solscan API would pull
    mock_trades = [
        {"timestamp": "2026-03-01T10:00:00", "wallet": "0xWASH1", "amount_usd": 50000, "is_circular": True},
        {"timestamp": "2026-03-01T11:00:00", "wallet": "0xREAL9", "amount_usd": 2000, "is_circular": False},
        {"timestamp": "2026-03-02T14:00:00", "wallet": "0xWASH2", "amount_usd": 75000, "is_circular": True},
    ]
    result = analyze_volume_manipulation("CRM_TOKEN", mock_trades)
    print(result['telegram_report'])

Part 2: The OSINT Arsenal (Clone these to your Contabo Server)
These are the actual software tools. You will use the git clone command on your Contabo server to install these inside your War Room. Your Python scripts (like osint.py) will trigger these tools automatically.

Sherlock (sherlock-project/sherlock):

You are thinking of Amazon Neptune.

When you hear about forensic firms mapping out massive syndicates, or Chainalysis drawing a web of hundreds of wallets, they are not using standard spreadsheets or pie charts. They are using Graph Databases, and Amazon Neptune is the AWS flagship service for exactly this.

If your database is just rows and columns, it is very hard for an AI to see the big picture. Amazon Neptune is specifically designed to store data as an interconnected web (a graph).

Here is how Neptune works for your $CRM War Room:

1. The "Nodes" and "Edges" (The Spiderweb)
Instead of tables, Neptune thinks in terms of entities (Nodes) and the connections between them (Edges).

Nodes: @CRM_Dev (Telegram Handle), 0xABC (Wallet), 192.168.1.1 (IP Address), Sosana (Project).

Edges: 0xABC [FUNDED_BY] Binance_Hot_Wallet. @CRM_Dev [DEPLOYED] Sosana.

2. The Auto-Linker
When your Python scripts (like osint.py and hops.py) run, they don't just dump the text report; they push the data directly into Neptune. Neptune automatically connects the dots. If the IP address you scraped from the $CRM website matches the IP address from the old Sosana scam, Neptune automatically draws a thick red line between them without you having to ask.

3. The Visual Output (Graph-Notebooks)
Neptune itself is the "brain," but to actually see the visual spiderweb, you use Amazon Neptune Graph Notebooks (which runs on Amazon SageMaker).

When you hit the end of your investigation, you run a query like: "Show me the shortest path between the CRM Deployer and any known KuCoin deposit address."

The notebook will instantly generate an interactive, drag-and-drop 3D visual graph. You can literally click and drag the nodes around to see how the scammer moved the money through 15 different ghost wallets before cashing out. You can export this visual directly as an image and send it to your Telegram bot.

Why this changes the game for you:
Traditional databases make you ask: "Who did Wallet A send money to?" then "Who did Wallet B send money to?" (which takes forever).
Neptune lets you ask: "Show me the entire criminal network connected to this one Discord message."

It is the absolute gold standard for Entity Resolution and tracing money laundering.

To bridge this into your current setup, do you want to keep the architecture lightweight for now and just use a free Python library (like NetworkX) to draw basic visual graphs directly in Telegram, or do you want to provision an Amazon Neptune database to handle the enterprise-grade web mapping?

It is extremely smart of you to check this before turning anything on. Cloud databases can become a massive black hole for money if you aren't careful.

To answer your question directly: No, it will not cost you out of pocket immediately. Between your $200 credit and the AWS Free Tier, you have a solid runway to build this War Room and map out the CRM syndicate without spending your own money.

However, we need to be very strategic about how you use that $200, because Amazon S3 and Amazon Neptune are in entirely different weight classes when it comes to pricing.

Here is the exact reality check on the costs:

1. Amazon S3 (The Data Lake)
The Cost: Practically invisible. AWS gives you 5GB for free every month. Even if you dump 100GB of chat logs, CSVs, and PDFs into S3, it costs roughly $2.30 per month.

The Verdict: Your $200 credit will cover S3 for years.

2. Amazon Neptune (The Graph Database)
This is where you have to be careful. Neptune is enterprise-grade software. It does not have a permanent "always free" tier like some other Amazon services.

The Free Trial (Month 1): If you have never used Neptune before, AWS gives you 30 days completely free (750 hours of a db.t4g.medium instance). You won't touch a penny of your $200 credit for the first month.

The Credit Burn (Month 2 and 3): Once the 30-day free trial ends, Neptune starts billing by the hour. A basic instance will burn through roughly $70 to $80 per month.

The Verdict: Your $200 credit will comfortably pay for Neptune for about 2 to 3 months after your free trial ends. Once the credit is gone, you will be paying roughly $70+ a month out of pocket.

How to game the system (The Investigator's Playbook)
Since you only need to crack this specific $CRM scam right now, you can use the "Spin and Kill" tactic to protect your $200 credit:

Keep your data permanently stored in S3 and ChromaDB on your cheap Contabo server.

When you have enough evidence and need to draw the massive syndicate spiderweb, you spin up your 30-day free trial of Neptune.

Have your Python script dump all the connections into Neptune, generate your 3D interactive graphs, and take your screenshots for the final report.

Crucial Step: Once the report is generated, you completely delete the Neptune instance.

By doing this, you extract the maximum value out of Amazon's enterprise tech to catch the scammers, and then you shut it off before it ever eats your $200 credit. You can save that credit for other tools, like AWS Textract to pull text out of thousands of screenshot images.

If you don't want to deal with the stress of a ticking billing clock on Neptune, we can stick to the free, lightweight Python approach (NetworkX library). It draws great spiderwebs directly in your Telegram bot for exactly $0 forever.

Do you want to play it safe and code the free Python graphing tool, or do you want to unleash the 30-day free trial of Neptune to build the 3D interactive map?

If we are going to use the full arsenal—MistTrack for AML, Chainabuse for global scam registries, Solscan for token graphs, Helius/QuickNode for RPC, and Groq/OpenRouter for the AI matrix—we need to completely armor the code.

I have refactored the architecture into Rug Munch Intelligence: Armored Node V38.0.

Major Architectural Upgrades:

The SecurityVault Class: APIs are no longer scattered. They are isolated in a dedicated vault that performs a pre-flight "ping" to every endpoint on startup. If an API is dead, it flags it red but keeps the bot alive.

Resilient Async Fetching: Every single API call is wrapped in a try/except block with a 15-second timeout. If Solscan rate-limits you, or Chainabuse drops a packet, the bot won't crash; it will return "API Timeout/Error" for that specific vector and continue processing the rest.

Full Arsenal Re-Integration: When you query a wallet, it now officially fires all 5 forensic APIs simultaneously (Helius, QuickNode, MistTrack, Chainabuse, Solscan) before feeding the payload to the multi-model AI.

Groq Speed Layer: I added Groq (Llama 3 70b) as the "Triage" model. It handles rapid data structuring because it processes at 800+ tokens a second, leaving the heavy logical deduction to the OpenRouter models.

async def safe_fetch(self, url, headers=None, payload=None, method="GET"):

"""Wrapper to prevent API crashes from taking down the bot."""

try:

async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as s:

if method == "GET":

async with s.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=12) as r:

return await r.json() if r.status == 200 else {"error": f"HTTP {r.status}"}

else:

async with s.post(url, headers=headers, json=payload, timeout=12) as r:

return await r.json() if r.status == 200 else {"error": f"HTTP {r.status}"}

except asyncio.TimeoutError:

return {"error": "API Timeout (>12s)"}

except Exception as e:

return {"error": f"Exception: {str(e)}"}

q_payload = {"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": "getAccountInfo", "params": [address, {"encoding": "base58"}]}

tasks.append(self.safe_fetch(self.vault.keys["quicknode_rpc"], payload=q_payload, method="POST"))


# 3. MistTrack

m_url = f"https://openapi.misttrack.io/v1/address_risk?coin=SOL&address={address}&api_key={self.vault.keys['misttrack']}"

tasks.append(self.safe_fetch(m_url))


# 4. Chainabuse

tasks.append(self.safe_fetch(c_url, headers={"Authorization": f"Basic {self.vault.keys['chainabuse']}"}))



results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

"""Manages all API integrations with rate limiting and error handling"""


def __init__(self):

self.rate_limits = {}

self.error_counts = {}

self.session = None


async def __aenter__(self):

self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession()

return self


async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):

if self.session:

await self.session.close()


async def make_request(self, api_name: str, url: str, method: str = "GET",

headers: Dict = None, params: Dict = None,

data: Dict = None, max_retries: int = 3) -> Dict:

"""Make API request with rate limiting and retry logic"""


# Check rate limits

if api_name in self.rate_limits:

wait_time = self.rate_limits[api_name] - time.time()

if wait_time > 0:

await asyncio.sleep(wait_time)


for attempt in range(max_retries):

try:

start_time = time.time()


if method.upper() == "GET":

async with self.session.get(url, headers=headers, params=params) as response:

response_time = time.time() - start_time

result = await response.json()


# Update rate limits

if 'X-RateLimit-Remaining' in response.headers:

remaining = int(response.headers['X-RateLimit-Remaining'])

if remaining < 5:

reset_time = int(response.headers.get('X-RateLimit-Reset', time.time() + 60))

self.rate_limits[api_name] = reset_time


# Log API usage

self.log_api_usage(api_name, url, response.status, response_time)


if response.status == 200:

self.error_counts[api_name] = 0

return {"success": True, "data": result, "api_source": api_name}

else:

return {"success": False, "error": f"HTTP {response.status}", "api_source": api_name}


elif method.upper() == "POST":

async with self.session.post(url, headers=headers, json=data) as response:

response_time = time.time() - start_time

result = await response.json()


self.log_api_usage(api_name, url, response.status, response_time)


if response.status in [200, 201]:

self.error_counts[api_name] = 0

return {"success": True, "data": result, "api_source": api_name}

else:

return {"success": False, "error": f"HTTP {response.status}", "api_source": api_name}


except Exception as e:

logging.error(f"API request failed (attempt {attempt + 1}): {e}")

self.error_counts[api_name] = self.error_counts.get(api_name, 0) + 1


if attempt < max_retries - 1:

await asyncio.sleep(2 ** attempt) # Exponential backoff

else:

return {"success": False, "error": str(e), "api_source": api_name}


def log_api_usage(self, api_name: str, endpoint: str, status_code: int, response_time: float):

"""Log API usage for monitoring"""

try:

conn = sqlite3.connect("forensic_data.db")

cursor = conn.cursor()


cursor.execute('''

INSERT INTO api_usage (api_name, endpoint, status_code, response_time)

VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)

''', (api_name, endpoint, status_code, response_time))


conn.commit()

conn.close()

except Exception as e:

logging.error(f"API usage logging error: {e}")# ==================== FORENSIC ANALYSIS ENGINE ====================class ForensicAnalysisEngine:

}# Cost tracking@dataclassclass CostTracker:

total_spent: float = 0.0

requests_made: int = 0

average_cost: float = 0.0

daily_budget: float = 50.0 # $50 daily budget

current_day: str = ""

daily_spent: float = 0.0class BotSwitchingManager:

"""Intelligent bot switching manager"""


def __init__(self):

self.cost_tracker = CostTracker()

self.request_history = []

self.performance_metrics = {}

self.current_day = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d")


def select_optimal_bot(self, task_type: str, complexity: str = "medium",

urgency: str = "normal", budget_constraint: Optional[float] = None) -> str:

"""Select the optimal bot for the task"""


# Determine role based on task

role = self.determine_role(task_type, complexity)


# Get candidates for role

candidates = [bot for bot in BOT_CONFIGS.values() if bot.role == role]


# Filter by budget if specified

if budget_constraint:

candidates = [bot for bot in candidates

if bot.cost_per_1k_input <= budget_constraint]


# Score each candidate

scored_candidates = []

for bot in candidates:

score = self.calculate_bot_score(bot, urgency, complexity)

scored_candidates.append((bot, score))


# Sort by score and select best

scored_candidates.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)


if scored_candidates:

selected_bot = scored_candidates[0][0]

logging.info(f"Selected {selected_bot.model_id} for {task_type} (role: {role.value})")

return selected_bot.model_id

else:

# Fallback to cheapest available

cheapest = min(candidates, key=lambda x: x.cost_per_1k_input)

return cheapest.model_id


def determine_role(self, task_type: str, complexity: str) -> BotRole:

"""Determine which bot role should handle the task"""

task_lower = task_type.lower()


# Quick responses and status updates

if any(word in task_lower for word in ["status", "report", "update", "quick", "simple"]):

return BotRole.REPORTER


# Code generation and debugging

elif any(word in task_lower for word in ["code", "debug", "script", "program", "function"]):

return BotRole.CODER


# Deep analysis and patterns

elif any(word in task_lower for word in ["analyze", "pattern", "forensic", "investigate", "deep"]):

return BotRole.ANALYST


# Final decisions and quality control

elif any(word in task_lower for word in ["judge", "decide", "final", "quality", "review"]):

return BotRole.JUDGE


# Data extraction and scraping

elif any(word in task_lower for word in ["scrape", "extract", "parse", "data"]):

return BotRole.SCRAPER


# Quick classification

elif any(word in task_lower for word in ["triage", "classify", "sort", "categorize"]):

return BotRole.TRIAGE


# Default based on complexity

else:

if complexity == "high":

return BotRole.ANALYST

elif complexity == "medium":

return BotRole.REPORTER

else:

return BotRole.REPORTER


def calculate_bot_score(self, bot: BotConfig, urgency: str, complexity: str) -> float:

"""Calculate suitability score for a bot"""

# Base score from quality

score = bot.quality_score * 0.4


# Adjust for urgency

if urgency == "high":

score += bot.speed_score * 0.3

elif urgency == "low":

score += (10 - bot.speed_score) * 0.2 # Prefer slower but more accurate


# Adjust for complexity

if complexity == "high":

score += bot.quality_score * 0.3

elif complexity == "low":

score += (10 - bot.cost_per_1k_input * 1000) * 0.2 # Prefer cheaper for simple tasks


# Budget consideration

remaining_budget = self.cost_tracker.daily_budget - self.cost_tracker.daily_spent

if remaining_budget < 10: # Less than $10 remaining

score += (10 - bot.cost_per_1k_input * 1000) * 0.3 # Strong preference for cheaper


return score


async def make_request(self, model_id: str, messages: List[Dict],

temperature: Optional[float] = None,

max_tokens: Optional[int] = None) -> Dict:

"""Make request to selected model with cost tracking"""


bot_config = BOT_CONFIGS.get(model_id)

if not bot_config:

raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {model_id}")


# Check daily budget

if self.cost_tracker.daily_spent >= self.cost_tracker.daily_budget:

return {"error": "daily_budget_exceeded", "suggested_alternative": self.get_budget_alternative()}


# Prepare request

temp = temperature if temperature is not None else bot_config.temperature

tokens = max_tokens if max_tokens is not None else bot_config.max_tokens


request_data = {

"model": model_id,

"messages": messages,

"temperature": temp,

"max_tokens": tokens

}


# Make request to OpenRouter

result = await self.call_openrouter(request_data)


# Track costs (estimate based on input/output tokens)

if "usage" in result:

input_tokens = result["usage"].get("prompt_tokens", 0)

output_tokens = result["usage"].get("completion_tokens", 0)


input_cost = (input_tokens / 1000) * bot_config.cost_per_1k_input

output_cost = (output_tokens / 1000) * bot_config.cost_per_1k_output

total_cost = input_cost + output_cost


self.update_cost_tracking(total_cost)


# Track performance

self.track_performance(model_id, result)


return result


async def call_openrouter(self, request_data: Dict) -> Dict:

"""Make API call to OpenRouter"""


headers = {

"Authorization": f"Bearer {OPENROUTER_API_KEY}",

"Content-Type": "application/json",

"HTTP-Referer": "https://your-app.com", # Your app URL

"X-Title": "Omega Forensic V5"

}


async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:

async with session.post(

"https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions",

headers=headers,

json=request_data

) as response:

if response.status == 200:

return await response.json()

else:

return {"error": f"API error {response.status}", "details": await response.text()}


def update_cost_tracking(self, cost: float):

"""Update cost tracking"""

self.cost_tracker.total_spent += cost

self.cost_tracker.daily_spent += cost

self.cost_tracker.requests_made += 1

self.cost_tracker.average_cost = self.cost_tracker.total_spent / self.cost_tracker.requests_made


# Check daily reset

current_day = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d")

if current_day != self.cost_tracker.current_day:

self.cost_tracker.current_day = current_day

self.cost_tracker.daily_spent = 0


def get_budget_alternative(self) -> str:

"""Get budget-friendly alternative when budget exceeded"""

# Find cheapest available bot

cheapest = min(BOT_CONFIGS.values(), key=lambda x: x.cost_per_1k_input)

return f"Budget exceeded. Suggest: {cheapest.model_id} (${cheapest.cost_per_1k_input:.4f}/1k tokens)"


def track_performance(self, model_id: str, result: Dict):

"""Track model performance metrics"""

if model_id not in self.performance_metrics:

self.performance_metrics[model_id] = {

"requests": 0,

"errors": 0,

"average_quality": 0,

"total_cost": 0

}


metrics = self.performance_metrics[model_id]

metrics["requests"] += 1


if "error" in result:

metrics["errors"] += 1

else:

# Estimate quality (simplified)

quality = 8.0 # Base quality

if "choices" in result and result["choices"]:

response_length = len(result["choices"][0].get("message", {}).get("content", ""))

quality += min(response_length / 1000, 2.0) # Longer responses = more detailed


metrics["average_quality"] = (

(metrics["average_quality"] * (metrics["requests"] - 1) + quality) / metrics["requests"]

)


def get_cost_summary(self) -> Dict:

"""Get detailed cost summary"""

return {

"total_spent": round(self.cost_tracker.total_spent, 4),

"daily_spent": round(self.cost_tracker.daily_spent, 4),

"daily_budget": self.cost_tracker.daily_budget,

"budget_remaining": round(self.cost_tracker.daily_budget - self.cost_tracker.daily_spent, 4),

"requests_made": self.cost_tracker.requests_made,

"average_cost_per_request": round(self.cost_tracker.average_cost, 6),

"cost_efficiency": "high" if self.cost_tracker.average_cost < 0.01 else "medium" if self.cost_tracker.average_cost < 0.05 else "low"

}


def get_performance_report(self) -> Dict:

"""Get performance report for all models"""

report = {

"model_performance": {},

"cost_efficiency": {},

"recommendations": []

}


for model_id, metrics in self.performance_metrics.items():

bot_config = BOT_CONFIGS.get(model_id)

if not bot_config:

continue


success_rate = (metrics["requests"] - metrics["errors"]) / metrics["requests"] * 100

cost_efficiency = metrics["total_cost"] / max(metrics["requests"], 1)


report["model_performance"][model_id] = {

"requests": metrics["requests"],

"success_rate": round(success_rate, 2),

"average_quality": round(metrics["average_quality"], 2),

"total_cost": round(metrics["total_cost"], 4),

"cost_per_request": round(cost_efficiency, 6),

"role": bot_config.role.value

}


# Add recommendations

if self.cost_tracker.daily_spent > self.cost_tracker.daily_budget * 0.8:

report["recommendations"].append("Consider switching to cheaper models for remaining tasks")


# Find most cost-effective bots by role

role_bots = [bot for bot in BOT_CONFIGS.values() if bot.role == role]

if role_bots:

cheapest = min(role_bots, key=lambda x: x.cost_per_1k_input)

report["cost_efficiency"][f"cheapest_{role.value}"] = cheapest.model_id

# core/database.py - Advanced Database Manager"""

"""import sqlite3import jsonimport loggingfrom datetime import datetimefrom pathlib import Pathfrom cryptography.fernet import Fernetimport base64import hashlibimport osclass DatabaseManager:

"""Advanced SQLite database manager with encryption and performance optimization"""


def __init__(self, db_path: str = "forensic_data.db"):

self.db_path = db_path

self.key = self._generate_key()

self.cipher = Fernet(self.key)

self._init_database()

self._create_indexes()


def _generate_key(self) -> bytes:

"""Generate encryption key from system fingerprint"""

system_fingerprint = f"{os.environ.get('USERNAME', 'unknown')}-{os.environ.get('COMPUTERNAME', 'unknown')}"

return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(hashlib.sha256(system_fingerprint.encode()).digest())


def _init_database(self):

"""Initialize database with all required tables"""

conn = sqlite3.connect(self.db_path)

cursor = conn.cursor()


# Evidence vault with encryption support

cursor.execute('''

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS evidence_vault (

id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,

wallet_address TEXT NOT NULL,

project_name TEXT,

evidence_type TEXT NOT NULL,

risk_score REAL,

confidence REAL,

raw_data TEXT,

encrypted_data TEXT,

timestamp DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,

api_source TEXT,

processing_time REAL,

UNIQUE(wallet_address, project_name, evidence_type)

)

''')


# Scammer profiles with behavioral analysis

cursor.execute('''

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS scammer_profiles (

wallet_address TEXT PRIMARY KEY,

entity_name TEXT,

risk_level TEXT CHECK(risk_level IN ('low', 'medium', 'high', 'extreme')),

psychological_profile TEXT,

geographic_data TEXT,

associated_wallets TEXT,

first_seen TIMESTAMP,

last_active TIMESTAMP,

total_victims INTEGER DEFAULT 0,

total_amount REAL DEFAULT 0,

evidence_score REAL,

updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP

)

''')


# Cross-project associations

cursor.execute('''

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cross_project_links (

id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,

wallet_address TEXT NOT NULL,

project_a TEXT NOT NULL,

project_b TEXT NOT NULL,

link_type TEXT,

link_strength REAL CHECK(link_strength >= 0 AND link_strength <= 1),

evidence TEXT,

timestamp DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,

UNIQUE(wallet_address, project_a, project_b)

)

''')


# API performance tracking

cursor.execute('''

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS api_performance (

id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,

api_name TEXT NOT NULL,

endpoint TEXT NOT NULL,

status_code INTEGER,

response_time REAL,

timestamp DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,

error_message TEXT,

cost REAL DEFAULT 0

)

''')


# Geographic analysis data

cursor.execute('''

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS geographic_analysis (

wallet_address TEXT PRIMARY KEY,

ip_data TEXT,

exchange_data TEXT,

risk_jurisdictions TEXT,

anonymity_score REAL CHECK(anonymity_score >= 0 AND anonymity_score <= 1),

extradition_possible BOOLEAN,

vpn_usage BOOLEAN,

tor_exit_node BOOLEAN,

updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP

)

''')


# Bot usage and cost tracking

cursor.execute('''

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS bot_usage (

id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,

bot_model TEXT NOT NULL,

task_type TEXT NOT NULL,

input_tokens INTEGER,

output_tokens INTEGER,

cost REAL,

response_time REAL,

quality_score REAL,

timestamp DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP

)

''')


conn.commit()

conn.close()

logging.info("Database initialized successfully")


def _create_indexes(self):

"""Create performance indexes"""

conn = sqlite3.connect(self.db_path)

cursor = conn.cursor()


indexes = [

"CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_wallet_address ON evidence_vault(wallet_address)",

"CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_timestamp ON evidence_vault(timestamp)",

"CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_risk_score ON evidence_vault(risk_score)",

"CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_project_name ON evidence_vault(project_name)",

"CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_last_active ON scammer_profiles(last_active)",

"CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_link_strength ON cross_project_links(link_strength)",

"CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_api_timestamp ON api_performance(timestamp)",

"CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_geo_anonymity ON geographic_analysis(anonymity_score)"

]


for index_sql in indexes:

cursor.execute(index_sql)

"""import aiohttpimport asyncioimport jsonimport loggingfrom datetime import datetimefrom typing import Dict, List, Optionalimport base64import reclass GitHubScamHarvester:

"""Harvest crypto scam data from GitHub repositories"""


def __init__(self):

self.session = None

self.repositories = [

# Major scam databases

"mr-guard/scam-database",

"CryptoScamDB/blacklist",

"etherscan/malicious-addresses",

"binance-chain/aml-scam-database",


# Specific blockchain scam repos

"solanamafia/scam-database",

"cardano-foundation/cardano-malware",

"polkadot-js/scam-database",


# Community maintained lists

"MetaMask/eth-phishing-detect",

"forta-network/scam-detector",

"chainalysis/cryptocurrency-scam-indicators",


# Research repositories

"MIT-Blockchain/cryptocurrency-scam-analysis",

"Cambridge-University/crypto-fraud-research",


# Exchange databases

"binance-exchange/binance-scam-database",

"coinbase/coinbase-malicious-addresses",

"kraken-exchange/security-research"

]


self.search_queries = [

"cryptocurrency scam addresses",

"bitcoin scam wallet",

"ethereum phishing addresses",

"solana rug pull",

"defi scam contracts",

"crypto honeypot detection",

"blockchain fraud analysis",

"cryptocurrency theft tracking"

]


async def harvest_all_repositories(self) -> Dict:

"""Harvest data from all configured repositories"""

async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as self.session:

tasks = []


# Repository harvesting

for repo in self.repositories:

tasks.append(self.harvest_repository(repo))


# Search-based harvesting

for query in self.search_queries:

tasks.append(self.search_github_repositories(query))


results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)


return self.consolidate_results(results)


async def harvest_repository(self, repo_path: str) -> Dict:

"""Harvest specific repository"""

try:

# Get repository contents

contents_url = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{repo_path}/contents"


async with self.session.get(contents_url) as response:

if response.status == 200:

contents = await response.json()

return await self.process_repository_contents(repo_path, contents)

else:

logging.warning(f"Could not access repository {repo_path}: {response.status}")

return {"error": f"Repository {repo_path} not accessible"}


except Exception as e:

logging.error(f"Error harvesting repository {repo_path}: {e}")

return {"error": str(e)}


async def process_repository_contents(self, repo_path: str, contents: List[Dict]) -> Dict:

"""Process repository contents for scam data"""

scam_data = {

"repository": repo_path,

"harvested_at": datetime.now().isoformat(),

"wallet_addresses": [],

"scam_contracts": [],

"metadata": {},

"files_processed": 0

}


# Look for relevant files

relevant_files = []

for item in contents:

if item["type"] == "file":

name = item["name"].lower()

if any(keyword in name for keyword in ["scam", "blacklist", "malicious", "phishing", "fraud"]):

relevant_files.append(item)

elif name.endswith((".json", ".csv", ".txt", ".md")):

relevant_files.append(item)


# Process each relevant file

for file_info in relevant_files:

try:

file_data = await self.process_file(file_info["download_url"], file_info["name"])

if file_data:

scam_data["wallet_addresses"].extend(file_data.get("wallets", []))

scam_data["scam_contracts"].extend(file_data.get("contracts", []))

scam_data["files_processed"] += 1


except Exception as e:

logging.warning(f"Could not process file {file_info['name']}: {e}")


return scam_data


async def process_file(self, file_url: str, filename: str) -> Optional[Dict]:

"""Process individual file for scam data"""

try:

async with self.session.get(file_url) as response:

if response.status == 200:

content = await response.text()

return self.extract_scam_data(content, filename)

else:

return None

except Exception as e:

logging.warning(f"Error processing file {filename}: {e}")

return None


def extract_scam_data(self, content: str, filename: str) -> Dict:

"""Extract scam data from file content"""

wallets = []

contracts = []


# Ethereum addresses

eth_pattern = r"0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}"

eth_matches = re.findall(eth_pattern, content)

wallets.extend([{"address": addr, "blockchain": "ethereum", "source": filename} for addr in eth_matches])


# Solana addresses

sol_pattern = r"[1-9A-HJ-NP-Za-km-z]{32,44}"

sol_matches = re.findall(sol_pattern, content)

wallets.extend([{"address": addr, "blockchain": "solana", "source": filename} for addr in sol_matches])


# Bitcoin addresses

btc_pattern = r"(bc1|[13])[a-zA-HJ-NP-Z0-9]{25,62}"

btc_matches = re.findall(btc_pattern, content)

wallets.extend([{"address": addr, "blockchain": "bitcoin", "source": filename} for addr in btc_matches])


# Contract addresses (more specific patterns)

contract_pattern = r"0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}"

contract_matches = re.findall(contract_pattern, content)

contracts.extend([{"address": addr, "type": "smart_contract", "source": filename} for addr in contract_matches])


# Remove duplicates while preserving metadata

unique_wallets = []

seen_addresses = set()

for wallet in wallets:

if wallet["address"] not in seen_addresses:

seen_addresses.add(wallet["address"])

unique_wallets.append(wallet)


return {

"wallets": unique_wallets,

"contracts": contracts,

"file_type": filename.split('.')[-1],

"content_size": len(content)

}


async def search_github_repositories(self, query: str) -> Dict:

"""Search GitHub for scam-related repositories"""

search_url = "https://api.github.com/search/repositories"

params = {

"q": f"{query} in:name,description,readme",

"sort": "updated",

"order": "desc",

"per_page": 100

}


try:

async with self.session.get(search_url, params=params) as response:

if response.status == 200:

data = await response.json()

return await self.process_search_results(data, query)

else:

return {"error": f"Search failed: {response.status}"}

except Exception as e:

logging.error(f"Error searching GitHub: {e}")

return {"error": str(e)}


async def process_search_results(self, data: Dict, query: str) -> Dict:

"""Process GitHub search results"""

repositories = data.get("items", [])


search_results = {

"query": query,

"total_count": data.get("total_count", 0),

"repositories_found": len(repositories),

"relevant_repos": []

}


for repo in repositories:

# Check if repository is likely to contain scam data

repo_name = repo["name"].lower()

repo_description = (repo.get("description") or "").lower()


scam_indicators = [

"scam", "fraud", "phishing", "malicious", "blacklist",

"honeypot", "rugpull", " theft", "steal", "hack"

]


relevance_score = 0

for indicator in scam_indicators:

if indicator in repo_name or indicator in repo_description:

relevance_score += 1


if relevance_score >= 2: # At least 2 indicators

search_results["relevant_repos"].append({

"name": repo["full_name"],

"description": repo.get("description", ""),

"stars": repo["stargazers_count"],

"last_updated": repo["updated_at"],

"relevance_score": relevance_score,

"url": repo["html_url"]

})


# Harvest from most relevant repositories

if search_results["relevant_repos"]:

top_repos = sorted(search_results["relevant_repos"],

key=lambda x: x["relevance_score"] + min(x["stars"]/100, 5),

reverse=True)[:5]


harvest_tasks = []

for repo in top_repos:

harvest_tasks.append(self.harvest_repository(repo["name"]))


harvest_results = await asyncio.gather(*harvest_tasks, return_exceptions=True)

search_results["harvested_data"] = [result for result in harvest_results if not isinstance(result, Exception)]


return search_results


def consolidate_results(self, results: List[Dict]) -> Dict:

"""Consolidate all harvested data"""

consolidated = {

"total_repositories": 0,

"total_wallet_addresses": 0,

"unique_wallets": set(),

"blockchain_distribution": {},

"source_breakdown": {},

"harvest_timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat()

}


for result in results:

if isinstance(result, dict) and "error" not in result:

if "wallet_addresses" in result:

consolidated["total_wallet_addresses"] += len(result["wallet_addresses"])


for wallet in result["wallet_addresses"]:

consolidated["unique_wallets"].add(wallet["address"])


blockchain = wallet["blockchain"]

if blockchain not in consolidated["blockchain_distribution"]:

consolidated["blockchain_distribution"][blockchain] = 0

consolidated["blockchain_distribution"][blockchain] += 1


source = result.get("repository", "unknown")

"""import aiohttpimport asyncioimport jsonimport loggingfrom datetime import datetime, timedeltafrom typing import Dict, List, Optional, Anyimport timefrom dataclasses import dataclass@dataclassclass GroqUsage:

total_cost: float = 0.0

requests_made: int = 0

average_cost_per_request: float = 0.0class GroqManager:

"""Manages GROQ API for ultra-fast analysis"""


def __init__(self):

self.api_key = GROQ_API_KEY

self.base_url = GROQ_BASE_URL

self.rate_limiter = asyncio.Semaphore(GROQ_MAX_REQUESTS_PER_MINUTE)

self.usage = GroqUsage()

self.request_times = []

self.error_count = 0


async def __aenter__(self):

self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession()

return self


async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):

if self.session:

await self.session.close()


async def rapid_analysis(self, prompt: str, model: str = "groq/llama-3.1-70b",

max_tokens: int = 2000, temperature: float = 0.1) -> Dict:

"""Ultra-fast analysis for high-frequency tasks"""


async with self.rate_limiter:

start_time = time.time()


headers = {

"Authorization": f"Bearer {self.api_key}",

"Content-Type": "application/json"

}


data = {

"model": GROQ_MODELS[model]["model_id"],

"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],

"max_tokens": max_tokens,

"temperature": temperature

}


try:

async with self.session.post(

f"{self.base_url}/chat/completions",

headers=headers,

json=data

) as response:


response_time = time.time() - start_time

self.request_times.append(response_time)


if response.status == 200:

result = await response.json()

await self._track_usage(result)

return self._format_result(result, model, response_time)

else:

self.error_count += 1

return {"error": f"GROQ API error {response.status}"}


except Exception as e:

self.error_count += 1

logging.error(f"GROQ request failed: {e}")

return {"error": str(e)}

"""Process multiple prompts in parallel - perfect for bulk analysis"""


tasks = []

for prompt in prompts:

task = self.rapid_analysis(prompt, model, max_tokens=500)

tasks.append(task)


results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)


# Filter out errors and exceptions

valid_results = []

for result in results:

if not isinstance(result, Exception) and "error" not in result:

valid_results.append(result)


return valid_results

# core/intelligent_switcher.py - Smart Bot Selection"""

Intelligent bot switching with cost optimization and performance tracking

"""import asyncioimport aiohttpimport jsonimport loggingfrom datetime import datetime, timedeltafrom typing import Dict, List, Optional, Anyimport timefrom dataclasses import dataclass@dataclassclass BotPerformance:

model_id: str

total_requests: int = 0

successful_requests: int = 0

total_cost: float = 0.0

average_response_time: float = 0.0

quality_score: float = 0.0

last_used: datetime = Noneclass IntelligentBotSwitcher:

"""Intelligent bot selection for optimal cost/performance ratio"""


def __init__(self):

self.performances = {} # Track bot performance

self.daily_budget = 25.0

self.daily_spent = 0.0

self.current_day = datetime.now().date()

self.request_history = []

self.learning_patterns = {}


async def select_optimal_bot(self, task_type: TaskType, urgency: str = "normal",

complexity: str = "medium", budget_hint: Optional[float] = None) -> Dict:

"""Select the optimal bot for the task"""


# Check daily budget reset

await self.check_daily_reset()


# Get available bots for task type

available_bots = [bot for bot in OPTIMAL_BOTS.values() if bot.task_type == task_type]


if not available_bots:

# Fallback to cheapest available

return await self.get_fallback_bot(task_type)


# Score each bot based on current conditions

scored_bots = []

for bot in available_bots:

score = await self.calculate_bot_score(bot, urgency, complexity, budget_hint)

scored_bots.append((bot, score))


# Sort by score and select best

scored_bots.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

selected_bot = scored_bots[0][0]


# Check budget

if self.daily_spent > self.daily_budget * 0.9: # 90% budget used

# Switch to cheapest available

cheapest = min(available_bots, key=lambda x: x.cost_per_1k_input)

if cheapest != selected_bot:

logging.info(f"Budget constraint: switched from {selected_bot.model_id} to {cheapest.model_id}")

selected_bot = cheapest


return {

"bot_config": selected_bot,

"selection_reason": self.get_selection_reason(selected_bot, scored_bots[0][1]),

"budget_remaining": self.daily_budget - self.daily_spent,

"performance_score": scored_bots[0][1]

}


async def calculate_bot_score(self, bot: OptimalBot, urgency: str, complexity: str, budget_hint: Optional[float]) -> float:

"""Calculate suitability score for a bot"""


score = 0.0


# Speed factor (urgency-based)

if urgency == "critical":

score += bot.speed_rating * 0.4

elif urgency == "high":

score += bot.speed_rating * 0.3

else:

score += bot.speed_rating * 0.2


# Quality factor (complexity-based)

if complexity == "high":

score += bot.quality_rating * 0.4

elif complexity == "medium":

score += bot.quality_rating * 0.3

else:

score += bot.quality_rating * 0.2


# Cost factor

cost_efficiency = 10.0 - (bot.cost_per_1k_input * 1000) # Lower cost = higher score

score += max(cost_efficiency, 0) * 0.2


# Performance history

if bot.model_id in self.performances:

perf = self.performances[bot.model_id]

success_rate = perf.successful_requests / max(perf.total_requests, 1)

score += success_rate * 2.0 # Strong weight on success rate


# Budget constraint

if budget_hint and budget_hint < 0.01: # Very tight budget

score += (10.0 - bot.cost_per_1k_input * 1000) * 0.3


return score

async def call_groq_api(self, model_id: str, prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 1000, temperature: float = 0.1) -> Dict:

"""Call GROQ API with optimal settings"""


headers = {

"Authorization": f"Bearer {GROQ_API_KEY}",

"Content-Type": "application/json"

}


data = {

"model": model_id,

"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],

"max_tokens": max_tokens,

"temperature": temperature

}


start_time = time.time()


try:

async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:

async with session.post(

f"{GROQ_BASE_URL}/chat/completions",

headers=headers,

json=data

) as response:


if response.status == 200:

result = await response.json()

response_time = time.time() - start_time


return {

"content": result["choices"][0]["message"]["content"],

"model": model_id,

"response_time": response_time,

"usage": result.get("usage", {}),

"api_source": "groq"

}

else:

return {"error": f"GROQ API error {response.status}"}

except Exception as e:

return {"error": str(e)}

else:

async def track_usage(self, bot_config: OptimalBot, result: Dict):

"""Track bot usage and performance"""


if "usage" in result:

input_tokens = result["usage"].get("prompt_tokens", 0)

output_tokens = result["usage"].get("completion_tokens", 0)


# Calculate cost

input_cost = (input_tokens / 1000) * bot_config.cost_per_1k_input

perf.successful_requests += 1

perf.total_cost += total_cost

perf.last_used = datetime.now()

# core/app.py - Main Application Orchestratorimport asyncioimport loggingfrom datetime import datetimefrom typing import Dict, List, Optionalfrom telegram import Updatefrom telegram.ext import Application, CommandHandler, CallbackQueryHandler, MessageHandler, filtersfrom telegram.ext import ContextTypesfrom config.settings import Settingsfrom core.database import EvidenceVaultfrom core.timeline import TimelineManagerfrom bots.investigator import InvestigatorBotfrom bots.bot_switcher import BotSwitcherfrom telegram.handlers import CommandHandlersfrom telegram.callbacks import CallbackHandlersfrom utils.logger import get_loggerclass OmegaForensicApp:

"""Main application orchestrating all components"""


def __init__(self):

self.settings = Settings()

self.logger = get_logger("OmegaForensicApp")


# Initialize core components

self.db = EvidenceVault()

self.timeline = TimelineManager()

self.investigator = InvestigatorBot()

self.bot_switcher = BotSwitcher()

self.command_handlers = CommandHandlers(self)

self.callback_handlers = CallbackHandlers(self)


# Telegram application

self.telegram_app = None


self.logger.info("✅ Omega Forensic V5 initialized")


async def run(self):

"""Run the complete application"""


# Build Telegram application

self.telegram_app = (

Application.builder()

.token(self.settings.TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN)

.build()

)


# Register handlers

await self._register_handlers()


# Start services

await self._start_services()


# Run Telegram bot

self.logger.info("🤖 Starting Telegram bot...")

await self.telegram_app.run_polling()


async def _register_handlers(self):

"""Register all Telegram handlers"""


# Command handlers

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CommandHandler("start", self.command_handlers.start_command))

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CommandHandler("help", self.command_handlers.help_command))

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CommandHandler("investigate", self.command_handlers.investigate_command))

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CommandHandler("analyze", self.command_handlers.analyze_command))

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CommandHandler("timeline", self.command_handlers.timeline_command))

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CommandHandler("suspects", self.command_handlers.suspects_command))

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CommandHandler("report", self.command_handlers.report_command))

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CommandHandler("note", self.command_handlers.note_command))


# Callback handlers

self.telegram_app.add_handler(CallbackQueryHandler(self.callback_handlers.handle_callback))


# Message handlers

self.telegram_app.add_handler(MessageHandler(filters.TEXT & ~filters.COMMAND, self.command_handlers.message_handler))


async def _start_services(self):

"""Start background services"""


# Start timeline reconstruction

asyncio.create_task(self.timeline.reconstruct_timeline())


# Start evidence collection

asyncio.create_task(self._collect_evidence())


self.logger.info("🔄 Background services started")


async def _collect_evidence(self):

"""Collect and analyze evidence continuously"""


while True:

try:

# Update wallet balances

await self._update_wallet_balances()


# Check for new transactions

await self._check_new_transactions()


# Update risk scores

await self._update_risk_scores()


await asyncio.sleep(300) # Every 5 minutes


except Exception as e:

self.logger.error(f"Error collecting evidence: {e}", exc_info=True)

await asyncio.sleep(60)


async def _update_wallet_balances(self):

"""Update balances for all tracked wallets"""


wallets = self.db.get_wallets()


for wallet in wallets:

try:

balance = await self.investigator.get_wallet_balance(wallet['address'])

if balance:

await self.db.update_wallet_balance(wallet['address'], balance)


except Exception as e:

self.logger.warning(f"Failed to update balance for {wallet['address']}: {e}")


async def _check_new_transactions(self):

"""Check for new suspicious transactions"""


# Get recent transactions for monitored wallets

suspicious_txs = await self.investigator.check_recent_transactions()


for tx in suspicious_txs:

if self._is_suspicious_transaction(tx):

await self.db.add_suspicious_transaction(tx)

self.logger.info(f"🚨 Suspicious transaction detected: {tx['signature']}")


def _is_suspicious_transaction(self, tx: Dict) -> bool:

"""Determine if transaction is suspicious"""


# Check for large amounts

if tx.get('amount', 0) > 1000000: # 1M+ tokens

return True


# Check for rapid transactions

if tx.get('time_window', 0) < 60: # Within 1 minute

return True


# Check for known scam patterns

if self.investigator.detects_scam_pattern(tx):

return True


return False


async def investigate_wallet(self, wallet_address: str, depth: int = 3) -> Dict:

"""Deep investigation of a specific wallet"""


self.logger.info(f"🔍 Investigating wallet: {wallet_address}")


# Get basic info

basic_info = await self.investigator.get_wallet_info(wallet_address)


# Get transaction history

tx_history = await self.investigator.get_transaction_history(wallet_address)


# Analyze connections

connections = await self.investigator.analyze_connections(wallet_address, depth)


# Risk assessment

risk_score = await self.investigator.calculate_risk_score(wallet_address)


# Timeline placement

timeline_position = await self.timeline.place_in_timeline(wallet_address, tx_history)


# Generate report

report = {

"wallet": wallet_address,

"basic_info": basic_info,

"transaction_history": tx_history,

"connections": connections,

"risk_score": risk_score,

"timeline_position": timeline_position,

"investigation_date": datetime.now().isoformat(),

"confidence": self._calculate_confidence(basic_info, connections),

"recommendation": self._generate_recommendation(risk_score, connections)

}


return report


def _calculate_confidence(self, basic_info: Dict, connections: Dict) -> float:

"""Calculate investigation confidence score"""


confidence = 0.5 # Base confidence


# Increase based on evidence strength

if basic_info.get('is_known_scam'):

confidence += 0.3


if len(connections.get('related_wallets', [])) > 5:

confidence += 0.2


if connections.get('suspicious_pattern_detected'):

confidence += 0.2


return min(confidence, 0.95) # Cap at 95%


def _generate_recommendation(self, risk_score: float, connections: Dict) -> str:

"""Generate action recommendation"""


if risk_score > 0.8:

return "IMMEDIATE_INVESTIGATION_REQUIRED"

elif risk_score > 0.6:

return "MONITOR_CLOSELY"

elif risk_score > 0.4:

return "CONTINUE_MONITORING"

else:

return "ROUTINE_MONITORING"

import sqlite3
import logging
from datetime import datetime

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class TimelineManager:
    """Manages the fuzzy chronological reconstruction from Aug 2025 - Mar 2026."""
    
    def __init__(self, db_path="crm_forensics.db"):
        self.db_path = db_path
        self.phases = [
            "Phase 1: Setup & Pre-Bonding (Aug 2025 - Oct 2025)",
            "Phase 2: The Trap & Extraction (Nov 2025 - Mid Mar 2026)",
            "Phase 3: Exposure & Panic (Late Mar 2026)"
        ]

    def categorize_date(self, approx_date: str) -> str:
        """Fuzzy logic to drop an event into the correct chronological phase."""
        date_lower = approx_date.lower()
        if any(month in date_lower for month in ["aug", "sep", "oct", "august", "september", "october"]):
            return self.phases[0]
        elif any(month in date_lower for month in ["nov", "dec", "jan", "feb", "november", "december", "january", "february"]):
            return self.phases[1]
        elif "mar" in date_lower or "march" in date_lower:
            return self.phases[2]
        return "Unknown Phase"

    def log_critical_snapshot(self, event_name: str, approx_date: str, description: str):
        """
        Special function strictly for your pre-announcement logs. 
        Tags this data as the definitive 'Baseline' before the scammers panicked.
        """
        phase = self.categorize_date(approx_date)
        with sqlite3.connect(self.db_path) as conn:
            cursor = conn.cursor()
            # Flags severity as "VINDICATION_BASELINE"
            cursor.execute('''INSERT INTO timeline (event_name, approx_date, severity, description)
                              VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)''', 
                           (event_name, approx_date, "VINDICATION_BASELINE", f"[{phase}] {description}"))
            conn.commit()
        logger.info(f"✅ Logged pre-announcement baseline evidence for {approx_date}.")

    def generate_timeline_report(self) -> str:
        """Pulls the chronological report for your Telegram bot."""
        report = "🕒 **$CRM MANIPULATION TIMELINE (Aug 2025 - Mar 2026)**\n\n"
        
        with sqlite3.connect(self.db_path) as conn:
            cursor = conn.cursor()
            cursor.execute("SELECT approx_date, event_name, severity, description FROM timeline ORDER BY id ASC")
            events = cursor.fetchall()

            for date, name, severity, desc in events:
                marker = "🚨" if severity == "VINDICATION_BASELINE" else "🔸"
                report += f"{marker} **{date}** | {name}\n   {desc}\n\n"
                
        return report
